metadata.title: Why a Cancer With Intact Tumor Suppressor Genes Still Behaves As If They Are Missing
metadata.purpose: Vox-explainer slate cut — Cancer Biology candidate 08. Mechanism: HPV E6/E7 inactivate p53 and Rb at the protein level without altering DNA sequence, making functional tumor suppressor loss invisible to genome sequencing.
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL #1F6F5C = intact / present / functional (genes, sequencing report, clean result); CRIMSON #BF3339 = lost / absent / broken (protein degraded, protein missing, functional inactivation); GOLD = single editor's-pen highlighter fill, once per graphic, never as text color; never swap mid-film

style_bible.visual_style: editorial paper collage on real newsprint scan; desaturated plates; flat annotation plane; serif labels with hairline underlines; hand-drawn annotation ring used once per graphic at most
style_bible.color_palette: cream ground, charcoal serif type, teal = intact/present/functional, crimson = lost/absent/broken, gold highlighter as the single editor's-pen voice

metadata.note: Card exclusions honored: NO full HPV life cycle, NO HPV vaccine mechanism, NO CIN staging, NO high-risk vs low-risk HPV type taxonomy, NO discussion of insertional mutagenesis or HBV/HCV. This film covers ONE mechanism only: E6/E7 inactivate p53 and Rb at the protein level without changing the DNA sequence. The discordance between sequencing and protein assay is the entire argument. The example (100 cervical cancers, IHC vs sequencing) is illustrative and labeled as such throughout.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B01: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B15: palette=claude but the outro is 'OutroCTA' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. The sequencing report comes back clean. TP53: wild type. RB1: wild type. Both tumor suppressor genes intact. This tumor should have functional p53 and Rb.

card.copy: Why intact tumor suppressor genes can still be functionally lost
card.sub: the gap between DNA and protein

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [TITLE] intact genes, yet lost function

beats.narration_text: HPV-positive cervical cancer biopsy. Two genes sequenced, both wild type throughout. No mutations. The lab is telling you nothing is broken at the DNA level.

props.headline: HPV-positive cervical cancer biopsy. Two genes sequenced, both wild type throughout.…

beats.scene_description: a printed genomics sequencing report pinned to newsprint ground — two rows labeled TP53 and RB1, both showing 'wild-type' in clean serif font; a green check beside each; slow push-in
beats.image_prompt: B02, printed genomic sequencing report pinned to aged newsprint, two rows: TP53 wild-type with green checkmark, RB1 wild-type with green checkmark, clean tidy laboratory report typography, desaturated editorial collage treatment, flat print reproduction
beats.new_visual_element: [STILL] the clean sequencing report — TP53 WT, RB1 WT

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [STILL] the clean sequencing report — TP53 WT, RB1 WT

beats.narration_text: Yet p53 protein is undetectable in this tumor. The cells cycle into S phase without restraint. Neither tumor suppressor is doing its job. The genes are intact. The proteins are gone.

production_viz.label: gene: intact vs protein: absent
production_viz.mechanic: two-row table: row 1 = TP53 gene (teal chip: INTACT) vs p53 protein (crimson chip: ABSENT); row 2 = RB1 gene (teal chip: INTACT) vs Rb protein (crimson chip: ABSENT)

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] gene intact vs protein absent — two-row discordance table

beats.narration_text: Both tumor suppressor genes are intact. Sequencing shows no mutations. This tumor should have functional p53 and Rb. Here neither protein is functional. Why does a clean sequencing report mask the actual loss of both tumor suppressors?

card.copy: Both genes intact. Neither protein functional. Why does a clean sequencing report mask the actual loss of both tumor suppressors?

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] THE QUESTION — on screen and in narration

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] THE QUESTION — on screen and in narration

beats.narration_text: The naive expectation is reasonable: tumor suppressor loss requires a mutation. Change the DNA, and the protein either disappears or stops working. Sequencing the gene tells you if it's been damaged.

production_viz.label: the expected path: mutation erases function
production_viz.mechanic: a left-to-right chain: DNA node (teal) → MUTATION (crimson) → protein node (crimson, absent) → function lost (crimson); 'sequencing detects this' teal label below the mutation

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] the naive model — mutation → protein loss → function loss

beats.narration_text: And for most tumor suppressors, in most cancers, that's exactly what you see. p53 mutated and non-functional. RB1 deleted. Sequencing finds the damage, sequencing explains the behavior.

production_viz.label: standard case: mutation present, protein absent, sequencing catches it
production_viz.mechanic: two columns gene / protein; gene = crimson MUTATED chip; protein = crimson ABSENT chip; teal bracket 'sequencing detects this'

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] standard case — mutation present, sequencing catches it

beats.narration_text: HPV brings a different playbook. The virus expresses two proteins — E6 and E7. Not mutations. Proteins. And proteins can act on other proteins without ever touching the DNA.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] section card — THE MECHANISM

beats.narration_text: E6 finds p53 protein inside the cell and hijacks the cell's own waste-disposal system — the ubiquitin machinery — redirecting it to tag p53 for destruction. The proteasome shreds p53. The gene is still there, still intact, silently producing p53 — and E6 destroys each copy before it can act.

production_viz.label: E6 redirects ubiquitin machinery to destroy p53 protein
production_viz.mechanic: gene box teal (stays intact); E6 diamond (crimson) → ubiquitin ligase → p53 protein circle → proteasome (crimson); transform: protein becomes destroyed

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] E6 hijacks ubiquitin, destroys p53 protein — gene stays intact

beats.narration_text: E7 targets Rb. Normally Rb holds the transcription factor E2F prisoner, and the cell can't start copying DNA until a proper growth signal releases E2F. E7 binds Rb directly, forces E2F loose, and the cell charges into S phase whether it should or not.

production_viz.label: E7 binds Rb, releases E2F, unlocks S-phase
production_viz.mechanic: RB1 gene box teal (stays intact); Rb holds E2F; E7 diamond (crimson) displaces Rb; E2F freed → S PHASE (crimson); transform animation

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] E7 displaces Rb, liberates E2F — gene stays intact

beats.narration_text: The DNA sequence is pristine. No mutation at TP53. No deletion at RB1. A sequencing assay looks only at nucleotides — it cannot see a viral protein destroying another protein. The functional loss is completely invisible to the assay.

production_viz.label: sequencing assay scope: DNA only — protein layer invisible
production_viz.mechanic: two horizontal bands: DNA LAYER (teal border) with intact TP53/RB1 chips; PROTEIN LAYER (crimson border) with absent p53/Rb chips; sequencing arrow stops at the layer boundary

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] two-layer diagram — assay reads DNA only, protein layer invisible

beats.narration_text: Here's an illustrative example. A team sequences one hundred cervical cancers. They find TP53 mutations in ten. The other ninety are wild type — so they conclude those ninety have intact p53. Then they measure p53 protein directly by immunohistochemistry. In seventy of those ninety, p53 protein is absent. The sequencing report said intact. The protein assay says gone.

production_viz.label: 100 cervical cancers: sequencing vs protein assay (illustrative)
production_viz.mechanic: isotype grid 100 squares: 10 crimson (TP53 mutated), 90 teal (WT); then 70 of 90 flip to crimson (p53 absent by IHC); 20 remain teal
production_viz.note: all numbers illustrative — labeled in narration and FACTCHECK

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] isotype grid — 100 cancers, sequencing vs IHC discordance

beats.narration_text: A clean sequencing report and absent tumor suppressor protein are not a contradiction. They are what protein-level inactivation looks like. The gene is a blueprint. E6 and E7 destroy the product before it ships. Sequencing reads blueprints. It cannot count what was built and destroyed.

production_viz.label: blueprint intact, product destroyed
production_viz.mechanic: gene box (teal) → production arrow → protein circle; E6/E7 node (crimson) destroys protein; gene stays teal/intact; gold highlighter bar under 'blueprints'

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] blueprint vs product — sequencing reads blueprints only

beats.narration_text: Intact genes. Absent proteins. A clean sequencing report masks the actual loss of both tumor suppressors — because E6 and E7 do their work at the protein level, without changing a single nucleotide. Sequence-level precision oncology would miss this entirely. CANCER BIOLOGY.

card.copy: Intact genes. Absent proteins. E6 and E7 inactivate tumor suppressors at the protein level — invisible to sequencing.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] endcard — question answered, topic kicker

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the Cancer Biology series.
